content.text: Why Deployment Topology Is an Architectural Decision

Deployment topology determines where each GenAI component runs — which cloud region, which network boundary, which hosting model — and therefore controls data residency, latency, availability, cost, and operational complexity. These are not infrastructure details to be delegated to a DevOps team after architecture is complete; they are first-class architectural decisions that constrain technology choices, compliance posture, and SLA targets.

A topology template codifies a pre-validated combination of component placements for a specific set of constraints. Instead of designing topology from scratch for every project, teams select the template that fits their data residency and availability requirements, then fill in project-specific values.

Topology decisions made late in delivery are expensive to reverse. The data residency requirement that forces on-premises LLM inference adds months of GPU procurement and operations work. Establish topology early, in the blueprint stage, not during sprint planning.

attrs.alt: Four GenAI deployment topology templates: A) Single-Region SaaS, B) Multi-Region Active-Active, C) On-Premises/Air-Gapped, D) Hybrid Cloud + On-Prem

content.text: All components run in a single cloud region. The application and vector store are enterprise-managed (e.g., on AWS us-east-1 or Azure westeurope). LLM and embedding calls go to SaaS provider APIs over the public internet with TLS, authenticated with API keys stored in a secrets manager.
Application data stays in chosen cloud region; prompt content sent to LLM provider's infrastructure
Single-region: regional cloud outage takes down the system
Lowest — single hop to cloud services, SaaS API calls add 100–500ms
Lowest CapEx; pay-per-token for LLM/embedding; storage costs for vector store
Lowest — managed services for all components
Internal tools, PoC-to-production path for non-regulated data, rapid iteration phase
EU data subject data requiring GDPR data residency guarantees with the LLM provider; highly regulated sectors without DPA

The full application stack — app layer, vector store, and LLM endpoint — is deployed in two or more regions simultaneously. A global load balancer routes each user request to the nearest region. Vector store data is replicated across regions (with eventual consistency or leader-follower replication depending on the store).
Each region keeps its own data; routing rules determine which users go to which region
Regional failover; if one region fails, load balancer redirects within seconds
Lowest end-user latency — users connect to the nearest region
Highest — all infrastructure duplicated; cross-region replication data transfer costs
Highest — configuration synchronisation, replication lag monitoring, split-brain prevention
Global user base with strict latency SLAs; critical applications requiring > 99.9% availability
Vector store data must be consistent across regions; index updates in EU region must reach US region before queries can use new documents

A practical consideration: if the LLM provider offers regional endpoints (Azure OpenAI in eu-west, us-east, etc.) that keep data within the region, use those rather than routing all LLM calls to one endpoint. This is a data residency prerequisite that must be verified with the provider and documented in the compliance checklist.

All components run in the enterprise data centre. The LLM is self-hosted on GPU hardware using a serving framework such as vLLM, llama.cpp, or Ollama. The embedding model and vector store are also self-hosted. No data leaves the enterprise network.
Complete — no data leaves the enterprise network
Depends on data centre HA; typically lower than cloud unless redundant DC investment made
Low for inference if GPU is on local network; high if GPU cluster is remote from application
High CapEx (GPU procurement); ongoing power, cooling, and GPU ops staff costs
Highest — model serving infrastructure, GPU driver management, model update pipeline all owned by the team
Top-secret / defence / intelligence data; regulatory requirement for full data sovereignty; GDPR scenarios where no SaaS DPA is achievable
Limited to open-weight models (Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen, etc.); GPT-4 and Claude are SaaS-only

# Template C: vLLM deployment on Kubernetes (GPU nodes)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: llm-inference
  namespace: genai
spec:
  replicas: 2
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: llm-inference
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: llm-inference
    spec:
      nodeSelector:
        accelerator: nvidia-a100  # target GPU node pool
      containers:
        - name: vllm
          image: vllm/vllm-openai:v0.5.0
          args:
            - --model
            - /models/meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct
            - --tensor-parallel-size
            - "2"            # 2 GPUs per replica
            - --max-model-len
            - "8192"
            - --api-key      # require auth even on internal network
            - $(VLLM_API_KEY)
          resources:
            limits:
              nvidia.com/gpu: "2"
              memory: "120Gi"
          volumeMounts:
            - mountPath: /models
              name: model-storage
          env:
            - name: VLLM_API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: vllm-secrets
                  key: api-key
      volumes:
        - name: model-storage
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: model-pvc  # ReadWriteMany NFS or local NVMe
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: llm-inference
  namespace: genai
spec:
  selector:
    app: llm-inference
  ports:
    - port: 8000
      targetPort: 8000
  type: ClusterIP  # internal only — not exposed outside cluster

The hybrid topology routes data based on classification: sensitive data (PII, regulated records) stays on-premises for LLM inference, while non-sensitive data uses a SaaS LLM for lower cost and higher capability. The orchestration layer runs in the cloud, where it has access to cloud-native services, and connects to the on-premises inference cluster via a private link or VPN tunnel.
Sensitive data stays on-prem; non-sensitive may go to SaaS
Cloud layer is highly available; on-prem layer has its own HA requirements
Adds private link round-trip; typically 5–20ms extra vs. pure cloud
Middle ground — GPU CapEx for sensitive path only; SaaS cost for general path
High — two infrastructure stacks, private link, data classification routing logic
Mixed sensitivity workloads; organisations migrating from on-prem to cloud; regulatory requirement for certain data classes only

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class DataSensitivity(Enum):
    PUBLIC = "public"
    INTERNAL = "internal"
    CONFIDENTIAL = "confidential"   # use on-prem LLM
    RESTRICTED = "restricted"       # use on-prem LLM + audit

@dataclass
class InferenceEndpoint:
    base_url: str
    api_key_secret: str
    max_tokens: int
    model: str

# Endpoint registry — filled from deployment config / secrets manager
ENDPOINTS: dict[DataSensitivity, InferenceEndpoint] = {
    DataSensitivity.PUBLIC: InferenceEndpoint(
        base_url="https://api.openai.com/v1",
        api_key_secret="openai-api-key",
        max_tokens=4096,
        model="gpt-4o",
    ),
    DataSensitivity.INTERNAL: InferenceEndpoint(
        base_url="https://api.openai.com/v1",
        api_key_secret="openai-api-key",
        max_tokens=4096,
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
    ),
    DataSensitivity.CONFIDENTIAL: InferenceEndpoint(
        base_url="http://llm-inference.genai.svc.cluster.local:8000/v1",  # on-prem
        api_key_secret="vllm-api-key",
        max_tokens=8192,
        model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct",
    ),
    DataSensitivity.RESTRICTED: InferenceEndpoint(
        base_url="http://llm-inference.genai.svc.cluster.local:8000/v1",  # on-prem
        api_key_secret="vllm-api-key",
        max_tokens=8192,
        model="meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-70B-Instruct",
    ),
}

def classify_request(prompt: str, user_context: dict) -> DataSensitivity:
    # PII detection, user role, document classification label
    if user_context.get("data_classification") in ("CONFIDENTIAL", "RESTRICTED"):
        return DataSensitivity(user_context["data_classification"].lower())
    if contains_pii(prompt):
        return DataSensitivity.CONFIDENTIAL
    return DataSensitivity.INTERNAL

def contains_pii(text: str) -> bool:
    import re
    pii_patterns = [
        r"\b\d{3}-\d{2}-\d{4}\b",          # SSN
        r"\b[A-Z]{2}\d{6}[A-Z]\b",            # passport number pattern
        r"\b\d{4}[\s-]\d{4}[\s-]\d{4}[\s-]\d{4}\b",  # card number
    ]
    return any(re.search(p, text) for p in pii_patterns)

def get_endpoint(sensitivity: DataSensitivity) -> InferenceEndpoint:
    return ENDPOINTS[sensitivity]
Only if LLM provider has DPA
Regulatory requirement for full audit log

Each topology template in the blueprint catalog ships with a corresponding Infrastructure-as-Code skeleton. The skeleton defines the standard module layout so that projects instantiate templates rather than writing IaC from scratch:

topology-templates/
  template-a-single-region/
    main.tf            # root module
    variables.tf       # project-specific inputs
    outputs.tf
    modules/
      app-layer/       # ECS / AKS application cluster
      vector-store/    # Aurora pgvector or Qdrant
      secrets/         # Secrets Manager + rotation
      networking/      # VPC, subnets, security groups
      observability/   # CloudWatch / Datadog integration
    environments/
      dev.tfvars
      staging.tfvars
      prod.tfvars

  template-b-multi-region/
    main.tf
    modules/
      region/          # parameterised per-region stack
      global-lb/       # Route 53 / Azure Front Door
      config-sync/     # cross-region configuration replication

  template-c-on-prem/
    helm/
      vllm/            # Helm chart for LLM inference
      qdrant/          # Helm chart for vector store
      app/             # Application deployment
    kustomize/
      base/
      overlays/
        gpu-a100/
        gpu-h100/

  template-d-hybrid/
    cloud/             # Template A modules for cloud layer
    onprem/            # Template C modules for inference layer
    networking/        # Private Link / VPN configuration
    routing/           # Data classification routing config
GPU Capacity Planning (Templates C and D)
On-premises GPU sizing requires knowing the inference throughput requirement before procurement. Key inputs:
Peak throughput target from the NFR table.
Average prompt + completion tokens. A 4 000-token average request at 70B parameters on A100 delivers ~20 tokens/second.
Prefill latency is roughly linear in prompt length; a 3 000-token prompt takes ~150ms on A100. Budget this against latency SLA.
vLLM's PagedAttention allows batching many concurrent requests. Effective throughput scales approximately linearly with batch size until memory-bound.

def estimate_gpu_count(
    peak_rps: float,
    avg_prompt_tokens: int,
    avg_completion_tokens: int,
    model_params_b: float,        # billions of parameters
    gpu_memory_gb: float = 80.0,  # A100 80GB
    target_latency_p95_s: float = 3.0,
) -> dict:
    # Rough heuristic: 2 bytes per param in fp16
    model_memory_gb = model_params_b * 2
    gpus_for_model = max(1, int(model_memory_gb / gpu_memory_gb) + 1)

    # vLLM throughput: approximately 1000 tokens/s per A100 for 70B model (fp16)
    throughput_per_gpu_tokens_s = 1000 / (model_params_b / 70)
    total_tokens_s_needed = peak_rps * (avg_prompt_tokens + avg_completion_tokens)

    gpus_for_throughput = total_tokens_s_needed / throughput_per_gpu_tokens_s * gpus_for_model

    # Add 30% headroom; round up to multiple of gpus_for_model (tensor parallel)
    gpus_needed = int(gpus_for_throughput * 1.3 / gpus_for_model + 1) * gpus_for_model

    return {
        "minimum_gpus": gpus_needed,
        "model_shards": gpus_for_model,
        "estimated_throughput_rps": throughput_per_gpu_tokens_s * gpus_needed / (avg_prompt_tokens + avg_completion_tokens) / gpus_for_model,
        "note": "Heuristic only — validate with load test before procurement",
    }

result = estimate_gpu_count(
    peak_rps=20,
    avg_prompt_tokens=3000,
    avg_completion_tokens=500,
    model_params_b=70,
    gpu_memory_gb=80,
)
print(result)
# {'minimum_gpus': 4, 'model_shards': 2, 'estimated_throughput_rps': 28.5, 'note': '...'}

The private link or VPN tunnel connecting cloud orchestration to on-premises inference adds latency. Measure baseline RTT across the link before the project committs to a latency SLA. A typical inter-DC private link adds 3–20ms. For a 3 000ms P95 latency target, a 20ms baseline RTT is acceptable; for a 1 000ms target it may not be.

The four topology templates cover the full range of enterprise GenAI deployment scenarios, from the simplest single-region SaaS model to the most controlled air-gapped on-premises deployment. Each template encodes the tradeoffs between data residency, availability, latency, cost, and operational complexity:
Best default for internal tools and non-regulated data. Fastest to ship, lowest cost.
Required for global user bases with strict latency SLAs or > 99.9% availability targets. Highest cost.
Required when no external data transfer is acceptable. Highest CapEx and operational burden; limited to open-weight models.
Best for mixed-sensitivity workloads. Sensitive data uses on-premises inference; non-sensitive uses SaaS. Balances cost and control.
Select the topology template in the blueprint stage, not during delivery. Late topology changes are the most expensive rework in GenAI project delivery.
